a:

If you did not knowingly consent to a loan, the entire transaction may be voidable. Additionally, check whether QuickPaisa appears on RBI's public directory of registered DLAs (rbi.org.in) — if it is not listed, it may be operating illegally.

q: My Kisan Credit Card was due for renewal in March, but now the bank is saying my account has become an NPA because I could not repay the full outstanding during the drought year. My crop was destroyed last year. Now they say I am not eligible for a fresh loan. How can I get a fresh loan when I have no money to clear the old one? This is creating a big problem.
a:
When crops fail due to natural calamities like drought, floods, or pest attacks, farmers have a legal right to avail of crop loan restructuring and rescheduling under the RBI's Prudential Framework for Stressed Assets. The RBI Master Circular on Agricultural Loans specifically mandates that banks shall not classify agricultural loan accounts as NPA purely on the basis of crop failure if the account was standard before the adverse event. Moreover, under the PM Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY), crop insurance claims should provide partial repayment. The bank is also required to explore the option of restructuring the loan for one additional crop cycle before moving it to NPA. Additionally, state governments routinely issue 'crop failure notifications' that activate special loan restructuring provisions at the State Level Bankers Committee level.

Step 1 - Obtain Crop Failure Certificate: Approach your Patwari or Tehsildar to get an official crop failure/natural calamity certificate specifying the percentage of crop loss.
Step 2 - Apply for Loan Restructuring: Submit a written application to the bank branch quoting 'RBI Master Circular on Relief Measures by Banks in Areas Affected by Natural Calamities' requesting restructuring and a fresh crop cycle.
Step 3 - Claim PMFBY Insurance: Verify if your KCC had PMFBY coverage — check via the official portal (pmfby.gov.in) or call 1800-180-1551

q: I want to know — if I take a personal loan from a bank and it becomes an NPA, can the bank put me in jail? My neighbour's husband, who has an overdue personal loan of ₹3 lakh, is scared of the police. Can the bank do this?
a:
This is one of the most important financial literacy questions in India, and the answer is clear: for an ordinary personal loan (unsecured), the bank CANNOT put the borrower in jail. Defaulting on a personal loan is a civil matter (a debt dispute), not a criminal offence. Under Indian law (and the Constitution), a person cannot be imprisoned merely for the inability to pay a debt. The bank's remedies for an unsecured personal loan default are: reporting to CIBIL (which impacts your credit score), filing a civil court suit for recovery, and potentially sending a legal notice — none of which result in police action or imprisonment. The only exceptions where loans touch criminal law are: if a fraudulent loan was obtained using fake documents (criminal fraud), if a cheque given for repayment bounces (Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act), or if assets pledged as security are fraudulently disposed of.

q: I am an elderly man with no income. My son who was paying my personal loan EMI died suddenly. The loan is in my name. Bank is calling me every day. I have no money to pay. I am 68 years old. Is there any protection for me?
a:
This is a deeply challenging situation. From a legal standpoint, personal loans are the liability of the borrower (you) and not automatically transferred to heirs unless the loan was jointly taken or secured against a specific asset. The bank cannot hold your son's estate liable for your personal debt unless he was a co-borrower or guarantor. For an unsecured personal loan, the bank's recourse in a default is limited — they can report it to CIBIL, pursue civil recovery proceedings, and make demands, but they cannot seize your primary residence (if it is not mortgaged) or personal belongings without a court order. If the personal loan had life insurance coverage (many banks attach loan insurance), a claim may be available. Many banks also have compassionate restructuring policies for elderly borrowers in extreme hardship.

q: I purchased a house under the PMAY Credit Linked Subsidy Scheme. The bank informed me that I would receive a subsidy of ₹2.67 lakh. It has been 8 months since my home loan commenced, yet the subsidy has not been credited to my account. What is the status, and how can I track it?
a:

Since 8 months have passed, this is beyond the usual timeframe; it likely indicates that the bank has not filed the claim on time, or the claim is stuck in verification. Normally, once the subsidy is applied, your EMI should reduce. If your EMI has not reduced after 8 months, the subsidy has likely not been processed.

q: What options are available for managing a hefty education loan after returning to India without securing a job abroad, especially when the EMI burden is eating up most of my current income?
a:
If your education loan repayment obligations are consuming most of your current income, you may explore options such as loan restructuring, refinancing, interest rate reduction, extending the repayment tenure, or securing additional income sources. Returning from overseas studies without landing a foreign job can create significant repayment pressure, but future income growth and career progression may improve affordability over time. Decisions involving the sale of essential family assets should be evaluated carefully, keeping in mind long-term housing needs and financial stability.

1. Discuss restructuring or tenure extension options with your lender.
2. Explore refinancing opportunities that might offer a lower interest rate.
3. Evaluate whether secured borrowing could reduce your effective borrowing cost.
4. Pursue higher-paying employment opportunities and focus on career advancement.
5. Seek freelance, consulting, or part-time income opportunities aligned with your existing skills.
6. Create a detailed repayment and cash-flow plan to prioritise essential expenses.

This way, you can understand potential strategies for managing a large education loan burden, including restructuring, refinancing, income growth, and long-term repayment planning.

q: I took a personal loan from an NBFC at 24% interest rate. After paying for 18 months, I want to prepay the full loan using my bonus. But the NBFC is saying I have to pay a prepayment penalty of 5%. Is this penalty legal? Can I avoid it?
a:
Prepayment penalty rules differ between floating rate and fixed rate loans. For floating rate personal loans, the RBI has prohibited prepayment penalties entirely for individual borrowers. For fixed rate personal loans, the RBI guidelines do not categorically prohibit prepayment charges, but require that they be disclosed upfront in the loan agreement and in the Key Fact Statement (KFS). A 5% prepayment penalty on fixed rate personal loans, while permissible if disclosed, is on the higher end — some NBFCs charge 2-3%. However, if the 5% penalty was not clearly disclosed in your original loan agreement or KFS, you have grounds to challenge it. The NBFC must prove the charge was pre-disclosed. Additionally, check whether your loan agreement specifies any lock-in period — many NBFCs allow penalty-free prepayment after a certain number of EMIs.

Check whether the 5% prepayment penalty was explicitly stated at the time of loan signing.; Step 2 - Check if Floating or Fixed Rate: Confirm whether your loan was disbursed at a floating or fixed rate. RBI prohibits prepayment charges on floating rate retail loans — zero charge applies if your rate was floating.; Step 3 - Negotiate with NBFC: Request the NBFC to waive or reduce the penalty. Many NBFCs waive or reduce it for full prepay

a:
As a 19-year-old with no credit history, you face the classic first-time borrower challenge. For a second-hand motorcycle, you are looking at approximately ₹40,000-₹70,000. q: I have a Jan Dhan account with SBI. The bank staff told me I could get an overdraft of ₹10,000 on the account. My account is 1 year old. But when I went to apply, they said I am not eligible and gave me no reason. I need money urgently for my daughter's school fees. What should I do?
a:
The Jan Dhan Overdraft facility of up to ₹10,000 (increased from ₹5,000 in 2018) is available to PMJDY account holders after 6 months of satisfactory account operation. The eligibility criteria are: account age of at least 6 months, at least one family member covered under PMJJBY or PMSBY, and satisfactory account conduct (regular transactions, not dormant). Only one OD is permitted per household. The common reasons banks refuse the OD are: the account has been dormant (no transactions), the ₹10,000 OD limit has already been used by another household member with the same bank, or the bank is misclassifying the account. A refusal without a written reason violates RBI's Fair Practices Code. The bank branch head has the authority to approve an OD up to ₹10,000 without further approvals — this product exists specifically for the urgent credit needs of low-income households.

Step 1 - Ensure Account Activity: If your account has had very few transactions, make 1-2 small transactions immediately to demonstrate recent activity before re-applying.
Step 2 - Request Written Refusal: Ask the bank staff in writing why the OD was denied. They are obligated to provide a reason.
Step 3 - Check Insurance Enrollment: Ensure your Jan Dhan RuPay card has been enrolled in PMSBY (accident insurance). If not, enrol at the branch. This is often a precondition banks look for.
Step 4 - Apply to Branch

a:
A SARFAESI notice after 8 years of regular repayment followed by a temporary job loss is a critical but recoverable situation. Under SARFAESI Act, after the initial demand notice under Section 13(2) is issued, the borrower has 60 days to respond and either clear arrears or propose restructuring. This 60-day window is your primary defensive space. The bank cannot take possession of the house before the 60-day period expires and the DM's authorization is received. Given 8 years of positive repayment history, SBI's risk appetite for foreclosure on this account is actually low — banks universally prefer resolution over property sale because property auctions are complex, slow, and often yield lower recovery. Your ₹2 lakh savings can fund a partial immediate payment plus 3-6 months of reduced EMI to demonstrate good faith.

q: Someone took a loan using my Aadhaar and PAN details without my knowledge and now CIBIL is showing a loan of ₹1.8 lakh in my name which I never took. My loan application for car got rejected because of this. What should I do?
a:
This is a case of identity theft resulting in a fraudulent loan entry on your credit report — a serious financial crime and regulatory violation. Under the Credit Information Companies (Regulation) Act 2005 and RBI guidelines, any credit account reported to CIBIL must have the borrower's verified KYC-authenticated consent. A loan disbursed without your physical or verified digital presence is inherently fraudulent, and the lender bears primary responsibility for KYC process failures. This constitutes a criminal identity theft offence under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) and the IT Act. Simultaneously, it is a CIBIL data accuracy dispute. Action must be taken on two parallel tracks: criminal complaint to police and a CIBIL dispute with the lender.

Step 1 - Obtain Full CIBIL Report: Pull your full CIBIL report (cibil.com) to identify the lender, loan account number, disbursement date, and outstanding amount of the fraudulent loan.; Step 2 - File Police FIR: File an FIR at the nearest cybercrime/police station under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) Sections 318 (cheating) and 319 (personation) citing identity fraud using your Aadhaar and PAN.; Step 3 - Dispute with Lender: Write a registered letter to the lender (whose name appears in CIBIL report) citing that the loan was disbursed without your knowledge or consent, and request immediate account closure and CIBIL rectification.

If you did not knowingly consent to the loan, the entire transaction may be voidable. Additionally, please check whether 'QuickPaisa' appears on the RBI's public directory of registered DLAs (rbi.org.in). If it is not listed, it may be operating illegally.

q: I am a small farmer in Jharkhand with 1.5 acres of land. I went to the bank to apply for a Kisan Credit Card, but they are saying my land is not in my name — it is in my late father's name and the mutation is pending. The bank refused to give me the KCC. What can I do? The moneylender is charging 36% interest for seeds and fertilizer.
a:
Under RBI and NABARD guidelines for KCC, tenant farmers, oral lessees, and sharecroppers are eligible for credit even without formal land ownership documents. A farmer with possessory rights backed by a revenue record, inheritance certificate, or a letter from the Panchayat can be considered. Banks cannot reject a KCC application solely because mutation is pending, provided the cultivator can establish possession and cultivation. The RBI has directed banks through its Priority Sector Lending Master Directions that 'small and marginal farmers' include all those cultivating land, regardless of formal title. NABARD's 2023 revision to KCC guidelines explicitly requires banks to accept joint liability and crop cultivation proof as alternate collateral.

q: My CIBIL score is showing 540 because of an old credit card I forgot about 5 years back. I paid a small balance of ₹800 but the bank reported it as 'written off' in CIBIL. Now I cannot get a home loan. How do I fix this CIBIL report?
a:
A 'written off' or 'settled' remark on a CIBIL report is one of the most severe credit derogatory markings and typically results in a score drop to the 500-600 range, making home loans nearly impossible to obtain. However, if you actually paid the outstanding balance of ₹800 in full and the bank still reported it as 'written off' rather than 'closed', this is a data furnishing error by the bank and is rectifiable. Under the Credit Information Companies (Regulation) Act, 2005 and RBI's guidelines on credit reporting, banks (Credit Information Members) are legally obligated to report accurate and updated credit information. If the actual amount was paid and the 'written off' status was not updated, you can dispute it through TransUnion CIBIL's Online Dispute Resolution process. The credit bureau is required to complete the investigation within 30 days of a raised dispute.

q: I am a tribal woman from Madhya Pradesh. I have no land in my name, no salary, I do small work like selling vegetables. I heard about MUDRA loan. Can I also get this loan without any papers? What is the minimum thing needed?
a:
Yes, you are exactly the target beneficiary for MUDRA Shishu loans (up to ₹50,000) under the Pradhan Mantri Mudra Yojana. The scheme is designed for non-farm micro-enterprises engaged in income-generating activities — vegetable selling fully qualifies. MUDRA Shishu loans are collateral-free, guarantor-free, and require minimal documentation. The basic documents needed are: Aadhaar card (identity proof), a photograph, and if available a bank account number. You do not need land documents, salary slips, or a formal business registration. Banks and MFIs enrolled under MUDRA are mandated to provide Shishu loans to such applicants. If you are a member of a Self Help Group (SHG), the loan access is even easier through the SHG-Bank Linkage route where the SHG acts as your guarantor collectively. Alternatively, Microfinance Institutions (MFIs) registered with MUDRA can process small loans faster than banks.

Step 1 - Join or Use Existing SHG: If you are already in an SHG, ask your SHG leader to apply for SHG-Bank Linkage credit, which makes you eligible for individual MUDRA-equivalent loans of ₹10,000–₹50,000.; Step 2 - Approach Nearest MFI: Visit a Microfinance Institution registered under MUDRA (like Ujjivan, Bandhan, or local NBFC-MFI) with just your Aadhaar card and a photograph.; Step 3 - Visit Common Service Centre (CSC): CSCs in rural areas can assist with MUDRA loan application

a:
An MSME loan missing 3 EMIs would typically transition to the SMA-2 (Special Mention Account) stage at 61-90 days overdue, and NPA after 90 days. Under the RBI's Prudential Framework for Resolution of Stressed Assets, banks must explore resolution options before initiating enforcement action on collateral. Specifically for MSMEs, the RBI had issued a dedicated one-time restructuring scheme for MSMEs with standard accounts as of January 1, 2020 (and subsequently for COVID-impacted MSMEs) allowing restructuring without NPA classification. Even outside special schemes, the RBI's regulatory framework requires a 'Resolution Plan' phase before collateral enforcement. Under SARFAESI, a 60-day demand notice must be given before the bank can take possession of secured assets — this is your window to negotiate restructuring.

a:

Once reactivated, the account needs to demonstrate 6 months of 'satisfactory conduct' before the overdraft facility can be extended. However, if the account was reactivated after the 24-month dormancy period, the 6-month clock restarts from scratch. The issue of 11 crore inactive PMJDY accounts (as reported in Dec 2024) is systemic — banks often don't proactively inform holders about dormancy and its impact on OD eligibility.

q: I am a vegetable seller on a footpath in Lucknow. I want to apply for PM SVANidhi loan but the bank is saying I need a Certificate of Vending which I don't have. The ULB says they haven't issued it yet to many vendors. What other option do I have to apply?

If the EMI was calculated based on an assumed higher salary, the bank can recalculate the tenure to bring the EMI within 40-50% of the salary. For instance, extending the tenure from 7 years to 12 years on a ₹12 lakh loan at 10% interest reduces the monthly payment from approximately ₹19,000 to around ₹13,000. q: I have deposited ₹5,000 in a fixed deposit at my cooperative bank. I also have an outstanding loan there. Can I break the FD to pay off the loan? Or will the bank adjust it automatically? I need ₹3,000 for an emergency, and the FD matures in 6 months.
a:
Banks generally have the legal right of 'set-off' — the ability to adjust a borrower's deposit against their outstanding loan if the loan is in default. However, for a non-defaulting account, the bank cannot unilaterally appropriate your FD without your consent. For your emergency need of ₹3,000, the fastest options are: (1) Take a 'loan against FD' — banks typically provide 85-90% of the FD value as an instant loan, keeping the FD intact and earning interest. This means you can get approximately ₹4,250 against your ₹5,000 FD immediately, repaying it by the FD maturity date; (2) Premature FD withdrawal — you can break the FD before maturity, usually with a penalty of 0.5-1% reduction in the interest rate, but getting the principal plus partial interest within 1-2 working days. The premature withdrawal option works for any emergency need.

Step 2 - Withdraw FD Prematurely if Urgent: If you need the money immediately and the FD interest penalty is acceptable, request premature closure of the FD at the bank. You will receive the principal + interest at a reduced rate (typically FD rate minus 0.5-1%).

a:

Once reactivated, the account needs to demonstrate 6 months of 'satisfactory conduct' before the overdraft facility can be extended. However, if the account was reactivated before the 24-month dormancy mark, the prior 6-month clock may restart. The problem of 11 crore inactive PMJDY accounts (as reported in Dec 2024) is systemic — banks often don't proactively inform holders about dormancy and its impact on OD eligibility.

The RBI's revised framework (2022) also requires all regulated MFIs to limit the monthly EMI across all MFI loans to 50% of the household's monthly income. This cap was specifically designed to prevent the debt spiral you are currently in.

a:

For fixed rate personal loans, the RBI guidelines do not categorically prohibit prepayment charges, but require that they be disclosed upfront in the loan agreement and in the Key Fact Statement (KFS). A 5% prepayment penalty on fixed rate personal loans, while permissible if disclosed, is on the higher end — some NBFCs charge 2-3%. However, if the 5% penalty was not clearly disclosed in your original loan agreement or KFS, you have grounds to challenge it. The NBFC must prove the charge was pre-disclosed. Additionally, check whether your loan agreement specifies any lock-in period — many NBFCs allow penalty-free prepayment after a certain number of EMIs.

If your bank is not cooperating despite your PM-Kisan enrollment, this is a specific compliance failure that can be reported.

q: I pledged my gold jewellery with an NBFC for a ₹60,000 gold loan. When I went to repay, they informed me that my gold had already been auctioned as I was 2 months overdue. I never received any notice prior to the auction. I had borrowed ₹60,000, yet they claim I still owe ₹15,000 even after the auction. This does not seem right.
a:
What you are describing is a clear violation of the RBI's Gold Loan Master Directions (June 2025), which mandate strict pre-auction notice requirements and transparency in the auction process. As per RBI regulations, before auctioning pledged gold, the lender must: (1) issue a written notice to the borrower, (2) provide a minimum notice period to allow for last-minute redemption, (3) conduct the auction at the taluka or district headquarters, and (4) return any surplus from the auction (sale amount minus outstanding dues) to the borrower within 7 working days. If you received no notice before the auction, this is a definite regulatory breach. Furthermore, if the auction proceeds were less than your outstanding loan—leaving you with further debt—it suggests either the gold was significantly undervalued at the time of pledging, or the auction was conducted at below-market rates. Both scenarios are valid grounds for filing a complaint.

Step 1 - Request Auction Documentation: Immediately write to the NBFC requesting the complete gold auction records, including the date, venue, bidder details, realised price, and a breakdown of how the proceeds were adjusted against your loan outstanding.

a:

The RBI found that some banks were charging interest from the date of loan sanction or the date the loan agreement was signed, rather than from the actual date of fund disbursement — resulting in customers paying for days when they had no use of the funds. This is a clear violation of interest charging principles and the RBI has explicitly directed all banks to charge interest only from the date of actual disbursement (credit to the beneficiary's account). You are entitled to a refund or credit for the 15 days of incorrectly charged interest.

If you are a member of a Self Help Group (SHG), getting the loan is even easier through the SHG-Bank Linkage route, where the SHG acts as your collective guarantor. Alternatively, Microfinance Institutions (MFIs) registered with MUDRA can process small loans faster than banks.

A Recovery Sardar (recovery agent) from the bank came to my house with 4-5 men, used abusive language in front of my family, and threatened to seize my tractor. a:
The conduct you are describing — arriving in groups, using abusive language in front of family, threatening non-permitted seizure (a financed tractor under KCC requires court process for repossession), and making false statements about 'putting family in jail' (creditor-debtor disputes are civil, not criminal) — violates the RBI's Fair Practices Code for Lenders and the Recovery Agents Guidelines in their entirety. Recovery agents cannot: visit in groups larger than 1-2 persons; visit outside 8 AM to 7 PM hours; use abusive or threatening language; threaten family members who are not borrowers; make false legal threats (family members cannot be jailed for a civil debt). These are not minor violations — they are grounds for the bank to lose its RBI license to use recovery agents, and the agents may face criminal charges.

Step 1 - Document Everything: Note names/vehicle numbers of agents, date, time, and witnesses. Record next visit if possible. Take photographs of any damage.; Step 2 - File FIR at Police Station: File an FIR at the nearest police station citing the abusive behavior, threats, and intimidation by bank recovery agents. This is criminal harassment under BNS.; Step 3 - Complain to Bank's Grievance Cell: Write a formal complaint to the bank's Nodal Officer (name and address on the bank's website) citing specific violations of the RBI Recovery Agent Code. a:

With monthly EMIs consuming a significant portion of income and living expenses absorbing the remainder, there is very little financial flexibility available. Debt consolidation can be beneficial if a new loan meaningfully reduces the monthly repayment burden, lowers the overall interest cost, and is used exclusively to close existing debts. However, obtaining a large unsecured loan may be difficult when the borrower's debt obligations are already high relative to income and employer verification requirements are weak. Taking additional funds beyond the amount needed for consolidation can increase financial risk if the extra cash is not carefully managed. Before seeking new borrowing, borrowers should evaluate restructuring options with existing lenders, longer repayment tenures, or conversion of credit card balances into structured repayment plans. Improving cash flow and reducing debt obligations should generally take priority over obtaining additional discretionary borrowing.

q: Can I make small part-payments on my education loan during the moratorium period to reduce future interest, or is there a minimum prepayment amount required?
a:
Borrowers often wish to make voluntary part-payments during the moratorium period of an education loan to reduce the outstanding balance and the future interest burden. The terms and conditions governing prepayments, including any minimum prepayment amount, are determined by the lending bank and the specific loan agreement. Before making a part-payment, borrowers should verify the applicable rules directly with the bank or review their loan documents. Even when EMI repayment has not yet started, understanding the lender's prepayment policy can help borrowers plan their repayments effectively.

1. Review the education loan agreement for prepayment and moratorium provisions.
2. Contact the lending bank directly to confirm any minimum prepayment requirements.
3. Request details on how voluntary payments are adjusted against the loan balance.
4. Keep records of all part-payments and communications with the lender.
5. Monitor the outstanding loan balance and accrued interest periodically.

q: I took a ₹2 lakh MUDRA Shishu loan from a bank to start my paan shop. Business did not run well during COVID and I could not repay for 1 year. Now the bank is calling my family members and threatening to take my household items. They are also saying they will put my name in CIBIL blacklist. What are my rights?
a:
The loan recovery conduct you are describing violates RBI's Fair Practices Code and Recovery Agent Guidelines, which strictly prohibit banks and their agents from contacting family members who are not co-borrowers or guarantors, threatening to seize household items without a court order, and harassing borrowers through intimidation tactics. For a small loan default, the bank cannot unilaterally seize household goods — this requires a court order under the applicable law (SARFAESI only applies to secured assets over ₹1 lakh, and MUDRA loans under ₹10 lakh are typically unsecured). Regarding CIBIL: default on any loan does result in a negative entry on the credit report, but the bank cannot 'blacklist' you arbitrarily — it must report factual repayment history. The COVID-19 period also featured special RBI restructuring provisions, and if you were not offered restructuring during 2020-2021, you may have a legitimate grievance.

Step 1 - Record Harassment: Note dates, times, and nature of threatening calls. q: I borrowed money from a local moneylender in my village at 48% interest per year for 3 years. The debt has doubled. Now I know about bank loans but bank says I have no CIBIL score. Can I use the bank loan to repay the moneylender and free myself? How does debt swap work?
a:
What you are describing — using formal institutional credit to repay high-cost informal debt — is called a 'debt swap' and is a well-established financial inclusion strategy. Multiple government schemes specifically support debt swaps for over-indebted rural borrowers. The SHG-Bank Linkage Programme explicitly allows SHG loans to be used for 'high cost debt swapping' — replacing moneylender loans with SHG group credit at much lower rates. If you are an SHG member, this is your fastest pathway. Alternatively, MUDRA Shishu/Kishor loans taken ostensibly for working capital can effectively replace high-cost moneylender debt, as the loan's use for 'business purpose' is broadly interpreted to include working capital that was previously financed by informal credit. For a 'No CIBIL' situation, SHG and MUDRA routes are specifically designed to work without credit history.

Step 1 - Join or Use SHG: If you are not already in an SHG, join one in your village and build 6 months of savings record. The loan recovery conduct you are describing violates RBI's Fair Practices Code and Recovery Agent Guidelines, which strictly prohibit banks and their agents from contacting family members who are not co-borrowers or guarantors, threatening to seize household items without a court order, and harassing borrowers through intimidation tactics. For a small loan default, the bank cannot unilaterally seize household goods — this requires a court order under the applicable law (SARFAESI only applies to secured assets over ₹1 lakh, and MUDRA loans under ₹10 lakh are typically unsecured). Regarding CIBIL: default on any loan does result in a negative entry on the credit report, but the bank cannot 'blacklist' you arbitrarily — it must report factual repayment history. The COVID-19 period also featured special RBI restructuring provisions, and if you were not offered restructuring during 2020-2021, you may have a legitimate grievance.

If the MFI is charging more than the stated 18% — RBI's MFI interest rate framework caps the maximum spread at 10% above the cost of funds, and the effective all-inclusive cost must be disclosed in writing — this may also constitute a regulatory violation of the Key Fact Statement (KFS) mandate.

The insurer's policy document and terms and conditions hold more weight than the lender's internal rules in determining refund eligibility. The new lender may also mandate a fresh insurance policy based on their underwriting norms. Apart from insurance, you must account for processing fees, legal charges, administrative costs, and documentation requirements specific to under-construction properties. For such properties, coordinating between the builder, the current lender, and the new lender is essential to execute fresh tripartite agreements and update financing records.

a:
Pre-approved loan SMS messages from banks are marketing communications, not automatic loan disbursements. No bank can disburse a loan to your account without your explicit, digitally or physically authenticated consent. The RBI's Digital Lending Directions 2025 explicitly prohibit auto-disbursal of credit without affirmative consent, and the Key Fact Statement (KFS) must be accepted before any disbursal. Ignoring the SMS is perfectly safe — it will not result in any loan being disbursed, and it will not affect your CIBIL score. What does happen in the background: when the bank generated this pre-approved offer, it may have done a 'soft inquiry' on your credit bureau data, which does NOT affect your score. Only a 'hard inquiry' (when you apply and the bank formally checks your score for a credit decision) affects CIBIL.

Step 1 - Ignore or Decline: Simply do not respond to the SMS. No loan is created by passive non-action. Your CIBIL score is safe.; Step 2 - Opt Out if Unwanted: Reply 'STOP' to the bank's promotional SMS or call the bank's customer care to opt out of loan marketing messages.; Step 3 - Understand Soft vs. Hard Enquiry: Know that a bank's internal review for a 'pre-approved' offer is a soft inquiry and is invisible on your consumer CIBIL report. Your father has every right to a complete account statement and a detailed interest calculation. The disparity between the ₹5,000 credit balance and the ₹18,000 loan outstanding likely means the ₹5,000 has not been adjusted against the loan repayment — which is itself unusual and worth investigating.

q: I am a 25-year-old who just got my first job. I have never taken any loan. My CIBIL score shows 'NH' which means no history. Every bank is rejecting my personal loan application saying no credit history. What is the fastest way to build credit score from zero?
a:
An 'NH' (No Hit) or '-1' CIBIL score means the person has never taken any credit and hence has no credit footprint. This is different from a bad score — it is simply invisible to lenders. The RBI Master Direction of January 2025 explicitly states that first-time borrowers with no credit history should not be automatically rejected solely on that basis; banks must perform wider due diligence. However, in practice, banks often avoid lending to NH individuals due to risk aversion. The fastest legitimate way to establish a credit history is through a secured credit card (card against fixed deposit) or a small secured loan, since the FD/collateral de-risks the lender. Once you start using and repaying on time, CIBIL begins recording your history and scores typically cross 700+ within 6-12 months of consistent responsible usage.

q: I have taken a home loan. I noticed the bank has charged interest from the date of loan sanction, but the money was actually disbursed to me 15 days later. I am being charged extra interest for 15 days that I shouldn't be paying. Is this legal?
a:
You have identified a systemic banking malpractice that was specifically flagged in the RBI's onsite examination findings published in its Ombudsman Annual Report for FY2023-24. The RBI found that some banks were charging interest from the date of loan sanction or the date the loan agreement was signed, rather than from the actual date of fund disbursement — resulting in customers paying for days when they had no use of the funds. This is a clear violation of interest charging principles, and the RBI has explicitly directed all banks to charge interest only from the date of actual disbursement (credit to the beneficiary's account). You are entitled to a refund or credit for the 15 days of incorrectly charged interest.

Step 1 - Verify the Dates: Obtain your loan statement from the bank showing: (a) Loan sanction date; (b) Date of first disbursement (credit to your/builder's account); (c) Date from which interest was charged. If (c) precedes (b), you have documented evidence of the malpractice.

Step 2 - Raise Written Complaint at Branch: Submit a written complaint to the branch manager requesting a recalculation of interest from the disbursement date and credit of the excess charged. Attach the loan account statement.

q: My Kisan Credit Card was due for renewal in March but now the bank is saying my account has become NPA because I could not repay the full outstanding during drought year. My crop was destroyed last year. Now they say I am not eligible for fresh loan. How can I get fresh loan when I have no money to clear old one? This is creating big problem.
a:
When crops fail due to natural calamities (drought, flood, pest attack), farmers have a legal right to avail crop loan restructuring and rescheduling under the RBI's Prudential Framework for Stressed Assets. The RBI Master Circular on Agricultural Loans specifically mandates that banks shall not classify agricultural loan accounts as NPA purely on the basis of crop failure if the account was standard before the adverse event. Moreover, under the PM Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY), crop insurance claims should provide partial repayment. The bank is also required to explore the option of restructuring the loan for one additional crop cycle before moving it to NPA. Additionally, state governments routinely issue 'crop failure notifications' that activate special loan restructuring provisions at the State Level Bankers Committee level.

q: I am a poor woman from Bihar. My name is in the PMAY-G list but the bank says my Aadhaar number does not match. I went to Aadhaar centre also but they said no mismatch on their side. How do I resolve this Aadhaar seeding problem in bank for getting my house money?
a:
Aadhaar-Bank account seeding mismatches are one of the most common DBT failure points for PMAY-G beneficiaries. The issue often involves minor discrepancies in name spelling between the Aadhaar database and the bank account KYC record — even a single character difference ('Devi' vs 'Devi ') prevents the match. The National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI) maintains the Aadhaar Mapper — the central database linking Aadhaar to bank accounts for DBT. Even if your UIDAI data is correct, if the bank uploaded your name with a typo in the NPCI mapper, the mismatch occurs at that level. The fix requires the bank to update your Aadhaar seeding in their system and in the NPCI mapper.

Step 1 - Check NPCI Aadhaar Mapper Status: Visit the NPCI website and use the NPCI Aadhaar Mapper lookup tool to check which bank account is currently linked to your Aadhaar for DBT purposes. The URL is https://resident.uidai.gov.in/bank-mapper.; Step 2 - Submit Aadhaar Re-seeding Form at Bank: Take a printed copy of your Aadhaar card to the bank branch and submit a written Aadhaar Re-seeding Request. q: I pledged my gold jewellery at an NBFC for ₹60,000 gold loan. When I went to repay, they said my gold has already been auctioned because I was 2 months late. I never got any notice before auction. I had borrowed ₹60,000 and they say I owe ₹15,000 more even after auction. This doesn't seem right.
a:
What you are describing violates RBI's Gold Loan Master Directions (June 2025) which mandate strict pre-auction notice requirements and auction process transparency. RBI regulations require that before auctioning pledged gold, the lender must: (1) provide written notice to the borrower, (2) give a minimum notice period allowing last-minute redemption, (3) conduct the auction at the taluka/district headquarters, and (4) return any auction surplus (sale amount minus outstanding) to the borrower within 7 working days. If you received no notice before auction, this is a clear regulatory violation. Additionally, if the auction amount was less than the outstanding loan (resulting in further debt), this suggests either the gold was significantly undervalued at pledging, or the auction was conducted at below-market prices — both of which are grounds for complaint.

a:

q: I am a marginal farmer and I have heard about Micro ATM and Business Correspondent. My village has no bank branch. I want to know if I can take a small loan from Business Correspondent in my village itself without going to the bank branch in town.
a:
Business Correspondents (BCs) — also known as Bank Mitras — are agents authorized by banks to provide basic banking services in unbanked and underbanked villages. BCs can perform: account opening, cash deposits and withdrawals, balance enquiry, fund transfers, and collection of small loan applications. However, a key limitation is that BCs typically CANNOT independently sanction or disburse loans — loan appraisal and sanction is done at the bank branch level, with the BC merely facilitating the application and collection. The exception is the PMJDY Overdraft (up to ₹10,000), which in some cases can be pre-approved and activated through the BC using biometric AePS authentication. For larger credit needs, the BC will help you fill the application and submit it to the main branch, reducing your need to visit the town bank.
